_meta.description: Tier 1/2 audit extension pack: phrasal verbs (phraseKind: 'phrasal'), B2/C1 vocabulary, idioms (phraseKind: 'idiom') and modern slang/contractions. Loaded on top of en.json by dictionary.ts. Translations are hand-curated, license: CC0.

look up.bilingual: (phrasal) buscar (información en un libro, diccionario, internet)
look up.monolingual: To search for information about something in a reference source.
examples: I'll look up his number in the directory.

look after.monolingual: To take care of someone or something.
examples: Can you look after the kids tonight?

look for.monolingual: To try to find something or someone.
examples: I'm looking for my keys.
I look forward to meeting you.

look out.monolingual: To be careful, especially of imminent danger.
examples: Look out — there's a car coming!
The police are looking into the matter.
Could you look over my essay before I submit it?
He really looks up to his older brother.

look down on.bilingual: (phrasal) despreciar, mirar por encima del hombro
examples: She looks down on her colleagues.
When I look back on those years, I smile.

give up.monolingual: To stop trying or to surrender.
examples: Don't give up — you're almost there.
She finally gave in and agreed to come.

give away.monolingual: To donate or to reveal a secret.
examples: He gave away all his books.
Please give me back my pen.

give out.monolingual: To distribute; or to stop functioning.
examples: My phone battery gave out.
They were giving out free samples.

give off.monolingual: To emit a smell, heat, or light.
examples: The flowers gave off a sweet smell.

get up.monolingual: To rise from bed or from sitting.
examples: I get up at seven every morning.
I get along well with my sister.

get away.monolingual: To escape or take a short break from work.
examples: The thief got away.

get away with.bilingual: (phrasal) salirse con la suya, escapar de las consecuencias
get away with.monolingual: To avoid punishment for doing something wrong.
examples: He cheated on the test and got away with it.

get back.monolingual: To return; or to receive something back.
examples: When did you get back from Paris?
We can get by on very little money.

get down.monolingual: To descend; or to make someone depressed.
examples: Don't let it get you down.

get in.monolingual: To enter a vehicle or arrive.
examples: What time did you get in last night?

get into.monolingual: To enter; or to become interested in.
examples: I've recently got into jazz.

get off.bilingual: (phrasal) bajarse (de un vehículo); salir del trabajo
get off.monolingual: To leave a vehicle; or to finish work.
examples: Get off at the next stop.

get on.monolingual: To board a vehicle; or to make progress.
examples: How are you getting on at school?

get on with.monolingual: To have a good relationship with; or to continue doing.
examples: Let me get on with my work.
Get out of here!
It took her months to get over the breakup.

get through.monolingual: To survive a difficulty; or to reach by phone.
examples: I couldn't get through to her.
Let's get together this weekend.

put on.monolingual: To dress in something; or to gain weight.
examples: Put on your coat — it's cold.
Don't put off until tomorrow what you can do today.
I won't put up with this behaviour any longer.

put away.monolingual: To place in a usual storage location.
examples: Put your toys away, please.

put down.monolingual: To place down; or to criticise harshly.
examples: He's always putting his sister down.
Please put out the fire before you leave.
I put together a small team.

take off.monolingual: To leave the ground (aircraft); or to remove clothing.
examples: The plane took off on time.
Take off your shoes.

take on.monolingual: To accept responsibility; or to hire.
examples: She took on too many projects.
A new manager took over the team.
I've taken up yoga.

take in.monolingual: To absorb information; or to give shelter.
examples: It's a lot of information to take in.

take out.monolingual: To remove; or to invite someone out.
examples: He took her out for dinner.

take back.monolingual: To retract a statement; or to return.
examples: I take back what I said.
I'll take care of the bill.
Turn on the lights, please.
Don't forget to turn off the TV.

turn up.monolingual: To arrive or appear; or to increase volume.
examples: She didn't turn up at the party.
Turn up the radio.

turn down.monolingual: To reject; or to decrease volume.
examples: He turned down the job offer.
The caterpillar turned into a butterfly.
It turned out to be a great evening.

turn over.bilingual: (phrasal) dar la vuelta; entregar (algo a alguien)
turn over.monolingual: To flip; or to hand over to authorities.
examples: Turn over the page.

turn around.monolingual: To reverse direction or rotate to face the opposite way.
examples: Turn around so I can see your face.
Come back soon!
Something has come up at work.

come up with.monolingual: To produce or invent (an idea, plan).
examples: She came up with a brilliant idea.

come across.monolingual: To find by chance; or to give a certain impression.
examples: I came across this book in the library.

come along.monolingual: To accompany; or to develop or progress.
examples: How is your project coming along?

come around.monolingual: To visit; or to change one's mind.
examples: He'll come around eventually.
Prices are coming down.
Please come in.
The button came off.

come on.monolingual: An exhortation; or to start operating.
examples: Oh come on, you can do better!

come out.monolingual: To be published; or to be revealed.
examples: His new album comes out next month.

come over.monolingual: To visit someone at their home.
examples: Come over for dinner tonight.

come up against.monolingual: To face a problem or opposition.
examples: We came up against strong resistance.
What's going on?

go off.monolingual: To explode; to ring (alarm); or to spoil (food).
examples: The alarm went off at 6am.

go out.monolingual: To leave home for social activity; or to date someone.
examples: Let's go out tonight.

go through.monolingual: To experience; or to examine carefully.
examples: She's going through a tough time.
Let's go over the plan again.
Go away and leave me alone.
I'd like to go back to Italy.
Go ahead, ask me anything.

go along with.monolingual: To agree to a plan or idea.
examples: I'll go along with whatever you decide.

make up.monolingual: To invent (a story); or to reconcile after a fight.
examples: He made up an excuse.

make out.monolingual: To perceive clearly; or to kiss passionately.
examples: I can't make out his handwriting.

make for.monolingual: To head toward; or to contribute to.
examples: The thief made for the door.
He bought flowers to make up for being late.
They broke up last summer.

break down.monolingual: To stop functioning; or to analyse in detail.
examples: My car broke down on the highway.
Someone broke in last night.

break out.monolingual: To start suddenly (war, fire); or to escape from prison.
examples: A fire broke out in the kitchen.

break off.monolingual: To interrupt; or to end an engagement.
examples: She broke off mid-sentence.
I need to find out his number.

figure out.monolingual: To understand or solve something through reasoning.
examples: I can't figure out how to use this app.

work out.monolingual: To exercise physically; or to solve a problem.
examples: I work out three times a week.

hang on.monolingual: To wait briefly; or to hold firmly.
examples: Hang on a second.
We hang out at the park.
She hung up on me.
They set up a new company.

set off.monolingual: To depart; or to trigger something.
examples: We set off at dawn.

set out.monolingual: To begin a journey; or to present in order.
examples: She set out to climb Everest.

set aside.monolingual: To reserve for a specific purpose.
examples: I set aside an hour for reading every day.
I ran into an old friend today.

run out.monolingual: To exhaust the supply of something.
examples: We've run out of milk.

run over.monolingual: To hit with a vehicle; or to review quickly.
examples: A car ran over the cat.
The boy ran away from home.

bring up.monolingual: To raise children; or to mention a topic.
examples: She was brought up by her grandmother.

bring back.monolingual: To return something; or to evoke memories.
examples: This song brings back memories.
The new law brought about huge changes.
Carry on with what you were doing.

carry out.monolingual: To perform or execute a task.
examples: They carried out the experiment carefully.

fill out.monolingual: To complete a form by adding information.
examples: Please fill out this form.

fill in.monolingual: To complete missing parts; or to substitute for someone.
examples: She filled in for me at the meeting.
Fill up the tank, please.
We checked in at 3pm.

check out.bilingual: (phrasal) dejar el hotel; echar un vistazo
check out.monolingual: To leave a hotel; or to examine something.
examples: Check out this new song!

drop off.bilingual: (phrasal) dejar (a alguien en un sitio); quedarse dormido
drop off.monolingual: To deliver someone/something; or to fall asleep.
examples: Drop me off at the station.

drop out.monolingual: To leave a course or activity before completion.
examples: He dropped out of college.

drop by.bilingual: (phrasal) pasarse a ver, hacer una visita corta
drop by.monolingual: To visit briefly without prior arrangement.
examples: Drop by anytime.
He didn't show up to the meeting.
Stop showing off your new car.

stand up.monolingual: To rise to a standing position; or to fail to keep an appointment.
examples: He stood me up last Friday.
Her work really stands out.
NASA stands for National Aeronautics and Space Administration.
Please sit down.
Wake up — it's almost noon!
I fell asleep on the couch.
My old bag is falling apart.

calm down.monolingual: To become less excited or upset.
examples: Calm down, everything will be fine.

cheer up.monolingual: To become or make someone more cheerful.
examples: Cheer up! It's not that bad.
Shut up and listen!

back up.monolingual: To support; or to save a copy of data.
examples: I'll back you up on this.
Back off — I need some space.

blow up.monolingual: To explode; to inflate; or to lose one's temper.
examples: The bomb blew up.

burn out.monolingual: To fail by overheating; or to suffer exhaustion.
examples: She burned out after two years in that job.

catch up.monolingual: To reach the same level or progress.
examples: Let's catch up over coffee.
Clean up your room!

count on.monolingual: To rely on someone or something.
examples: You can count on me.

cut down.monolingual: To reduce consumption; or to fell a tree.
examples: I need to cut down on coffee.
We were cut off mid-conversation.

deal with.monolingual: To handle or manage a situation.
examples: I'll deal with it tomorrow.

dress up.monolingual: To wear formal clothes; or to wear a costume.
examples: We dressed up for the wedding.

end up.monolingual: To finish in a particular way or place.
examples: We ended up missing the train.
He's fallen behind in his studies.

fall for.monolingual: To fall in love with; or to be deceived by.
examples: I fell for her at first sight.

freak out.monolingual: Informal: to become extremely upset or frightened.
examples: Don't freak out — it's just a spider.
I need to get rid of these old clothes.

hand in.monolingual: To submit something to an authority.
examples: Please hand in your homework.
The teacher handed out the worksheets.

hold on.monolingual: To wait briefly; or to endure.
examples: Hold on a minute.

hold up.monolingual: To delay; or to rob with a weapon.
examples: Traffic held us up for an hour.

keep up.bilingual: (phrasal) seguir el ritmo, mantener el nivel
keep up.monolingual: To maintain the same pace or level.
examples: I can't keep up with the news lately.
Keep on trying.
I don't want to let you down.

log in.monolingual: To sign into a computer system.
examples: You need to log in first.

log out.monolingual: To sign out of a computer system.
examples: Don't forget to log out.

mess up.monolingual: Informal: to spoil or ruin something.
examples: I really messed up the interview.
It's time to move on with your life.

move in.monolingual: To start living in a new place.
examples: They moved in last week.

move out.monolingual: To stop living in a place.
examples: I'm moving out next month.
My grandmother passed away last year.
She passed out from the heat.

pay back.monolingual: To repay a debt; or to take revenge.
examples: I'll pay you back tomorrow.

pay off.monolingual: To clear a debt; or to yield positive results.
examples: Hard work pays off.

pick up.monolingual: To collect; to learn casually; or to flirt with.
examples: I'll pick you up at eight.

point out.monolingual: To bring something to someone's attention.
examples: She pointed out the mistake.

ring up.monolingual: British: to call on the phone.
examples: I'll ring you up tomorrow.
We can't rule out the possibility of rain.
She's saving up for a new car.

settle down.monolingual: To establish a stable life; or to become calm.
examples: They want to settle down and have kids.
We'll sort it out tomorrow.

stick to.monolingual: To adhere to a plan or rule.
examples: Stick to the plan.

stick with.monolingual: To continue with a choice or person.
examples: I'll stick with my original answer.

split up.monolingual: To end a relationship or divide a group.
examples: They split up after ten years.
Can you speed up a little?
Slow down, you're driving too fast.
Let's talk it over tomorrow.
The teacher told me off for being late.
Take a few days to think it over.
Don't throw away those old letters.
He threw up after eating bad fish.

try on.monolingual: To wear clothes to test fit.
examples: Can I try on this jacket?

try out.monolingual: To test something to see if it works.
examples: I want to try out this recipe.
We've used up all the flour.

wait on.monolingual: To serve someone (e.g. at a table).
examples: She waited on us all evening.

warm up.monolingual: To heat slightly; or to prepare with light exercise.
examples: Warm up the leftovers.

wash up.monolingual: To clean dishes; or to wash one's face and hands.
examples: Could you wash up after dinner?

watch out.monolingual: Be careful, especially of imminent danger.
examples: Watch out for the ice on the road.
I'm working on a new song.

wear out.monolingual: To become unusable through use; or to exhaust someone.
examples: These shoes are wearing out.
Let me write down your address.
Zoom in on the bottom-left corner.

kick off.monolingual: To begin, especially a planned event.
examples: The festival kicks off this Friday.
I can't believe she pulled it off.

pull over.bilingual: (phrasal) parar el coche (al lado de la carretera)
pull over.monolingual: To stop a vehicle by the side of the road.
examples: The police asked us to pull over.
The exam was a piece of cake.

break a leg.monolingual: An idiom used to wish someone good luck, especially before a performance.
examples: Break a leg out there tonight!
I'm feeling a bit under the weather today.

once in a blue moon.bilingual: (idiom) muy de vez en cuando, casi nunca
examples: I only see him once in a blue moon.
I need to hit the books — exams start Monday.
I'm exhausted — time to hit the sack.

spill the beans.bilingual: (idiom) revelar un secreto, irse de la lengua
examples: Come on, spill the beans!

let the cat out of the bag.token: let the cat out of the bag
let the cat out of the bag.bilingual: (idiom) revelar un secreto (a veces sin querer)
let the cat out of the bag.monolingual: To reveal a secret, often accidentally.
examples: She let the cat out of the bag about the surprise party.

cost an arm and a leg.token: cost an arm and a leg
cost an arm and a leg.translation: costar un ojo de la cara
examples: This watch cost an arm and a leg.

the best of both worlds.translation: lo mejor de los dos mundos
the best of both worlds.monolingual: An advantageous situation combining the benefits of two different things.
examples: Working from home is the best of both worlds.

speak of the devil.monolingual: Said when someone you were just talking about appears.
examples: Speak of the devil — here she comes!

on the same page.monolingual: Sharing the same understanding or opinion.
examples: Let's make sure we're all on the same page.
She called me out of the blue.

in the long run.bilingual: (idiom) a largo plazo, a la larga
examples: This decision will pay off in the long run.

at the end of the day.token: at the end of the day
examples: At the end of the day, family is what matters.
By and large, the project was a success.
Forget it — no big deal.
No worries, I'll handle it.
That guy is a real piece of work.

rain check.monolingual: A promise to do something at a later date.
examples: Can I take a rain check on lunch?
He left the country for good.
In a nutshell, we need more time.

hit the road.bilingual: (idiom) salir de viaje, ponerse en camino
hit the road.monolingual: To leave or begin a journey.
examples: It's late — let's hit the road.

call it a day.monolingual: To stop working for the day.
examples: I'm tired — let's call it a day.

long story short.monolingual: Used to give a brief version of a longer story.
examples: Long story short, we missed the train.

you bet.bilingual: (idiom, coloq.) por supuesto, claro que sí
examples: "Are you coming?" "You bet!"
He passed the exam? No kidding!

back in the day.monolingual: In the past, especially during a remembered time.
examples: Back in the day, we used to walk to school.

the bottom line.monolingual: The most important point or final result.
examples: The bottom line is, we can't afford it.
That doesn't make sense to me.
Small actions can make a big difference.
Let's keep in touch.
We arrived just in time.

on time.monolingual: At the scheduled or agreed time.
examples: The train arrived on time.
I'll do it right away.
All of a sudden, it started raining.

as a matter of fact.monolingual: In fact, used to introduce a correction or addition.
examples: As a matter of fact, I do know him.

first of all.monolingual: Used to introduce the first point.
examples: First of all, let me thank you for coming.
I'm gonna call you tomorrow.
Do you wanna come with us?

gotta.monolingual: Informal contraction of "have got to" / "got to".
examples: I gotta go now.

kinda.bilingual: (coloq., contracción de "kind of") un poco, más o menos
examples: I'm kinda tired.

sorta.bilingual: (coloq., contracción de "sort of") más o menos
examples: He's sorta my boyfriend, I guess.
Lemme see that.
Gimme a minute.
I dunno where he is.

ain't.translation: no es / no soy / no estás... (informal)
ain't.monolingual: Informal negation of am/is/are/has/have (non-standard but very common in songs, slang).
examples: That ain't right.

y'all.monolingual: Southern US informal contraction of "you all".
examples: Y'all coming to the party?

achieve.monolingual: To successfully reach a desired objective.
examples: She achieved her goals through hard work.

acknowledge.monolingual: To accept or admit the existence of something.
examples: He acknowledged his mistake.

acquire.monolingual: To come to own or possess.
examples: She acquired a taste for olives.
Children adapt quickly to new schools.

afford.monolingual: To have enough money or time for something.
examples: I can't afford a new car.

appreciate.monolingual: To recognize the value of; or to be grateful for.
examples: I really appreciate your help.

argue.monolingual: To exchange diverging or opposite views.
examples: They argue about politics constantly.

arrange.monolingual: To put things in order or to plan.
examples: I arranged a meeting for Friday.

assume.monolingual: To accept as true without proof.
examples: I assumed you were busy.

attempt.monolingual: To try to do something; or an act of trying.
examples: She made an attempt to climb the mountain.

approach.monolingual: To come closer; or a method of dealing with something.
examples: We need a new approach to this problem.

aware.monolingual: Having knowledge or perception of a situation.
examples: Are you aware of the risks?

behave.monolingual: To act in a particular way.
examples: The children behaved well at the party.

blame.monolingual: To assign responsibility for a fault.
examples: Don't blame me — it wasn't my fault.
Sorry to bother you.

burst.monolingual: To break open suddenly and violently.
examples: The balloon burst with a loud bang.

claim.monolingual: To state as true; or to demand as one's right.
examples: He claims to know the truth.
She complained about the noise.

conclude.monolingual: To bring to an end; or to deduce.
examples: The meeting concluded at noon.

confess.monolingual: To admit something, especially a fault.
examples: He confessed to the crime.
I'll consider your offer.

convince.monolingual: To cause someone to believe firmly in something.
examples: She convinced me to stay.

decline.monolingual: To politely refuse; or to become smaller.
examples: She declined the invitation.

demand.monolingual: To ask forcefully; or a strong request.
examples: He demanded an explanation.

deny.monolingual: To refuse to admit the truth of.
examples: He denied any involvement.
You deserve a break.

encourage.monolingual: To give support, confidence, or hope.
examples: My parents always encouraged me.

establish.monolingual: To set up on a firm or permanent basis.
examples: The company was established in 1990.
The doctor examined her carefully.

expand.monolingual: To become larger or more extensive.
examples: The company is expanding into Asia.

expect.monolingual: To regard as likely to happen.
examples: I expect to arrive at six.

explore.monolingual: To travel through to learn about; or to investigate.
examples: We explored the old castle.
He failed the exam.

handle.monolingual: To deal with a situation; or a part of an object designed to be held.
examples: She handled the crisis professionally.

ignore.monolingual: To refuse to take notice of.
examples: Just ignore him.
She wants to improve her English.
The arrow indicates the exit.

involve.monolingual: To include as a necessary part.
examples: The job involves a lot of travel.

manage.monolingual: To be in charge of; or to succeed in doing.
examples: She manages a small team.

matter.monolingual: To be important; or a subject under consideration.
examples: It doesn't matter.
She mentioned your name.

notice.monolingual: To become aware of; or a written announcement.
examples: Did you notice the change?
She obtained a degree in physics.

occur.monolingual: To happen; or to come into the mind.
examples: It occurred to me yesterday.

overcome.monolingual: To succeed in dealing with a problem.
examples: She overcame many obstacles.

persuade.monolingual: To induce someone to do something by reasoning.
examples: He persuaded me to stay.

pretend.monolingual: To behave as if something is true when it is not.
examples: Stop pretending you don't know.
Exercise can prevent many diseases.

promote.monolingual: To support actively; or to raise to a higher rank.
examples: She was promoted to manager.
The hotel provides breakfast.

recognize.monolingual: To identify someone as previously known.
examples: I didn't recognize you with that haircut!
He refused to apologise.
I regret not calling her.

release.monolingual: To set free; or to make publicly available.
examples: The album was released yesterday.

remain.monolingual: To continue to exist or be present.
examples: Few people remained at the end.
Remind me to call mum.
I need to replace the battery.

represent.monolingual: To act or speak for; or to symbolize.
examples: The stars represent the states.

require.monolingual: To need for a purpose; or to demand.
examples: The job requires patience.
She didn't respond to my email.

reveal.monolingual: To make known something previously secret.
examples: He revealed the truth.
Nothing satisfies him.

settle.monolingual: To establish a home; or to resolve a dispute.
examples: They settled in Spain.

struggle.monolingual: To make a great effort under difficulty.
examples: She struggled to understand.

succeed.bilingual: (verbo) tener éxito, conseguir; suceder a alguien
succeed.monolingual: To achieve the desired result; or to follow in order.
examples: She succeeded in convincing him.

suggest.monolingual: To put forward an idea or plan.
examples: I suggest we leave now.

suppose.monolingual: To assume something as the case.
examples: I suppose you're right.

tend.monolingual: To regularly behave in a particular way.
examples: I tend to wake up early.
I can't tolerate rudeness.

transform.monolingual: To change in form or character.
examples: The internet has transformed our lives.

warn.monolingual: To inform of danger or a problem.
examples: I warned you about him.

withdraw.monolingual: To remove or take away; or to leave.
examples: I need to withdraw some cash.

ambiguous.monolingual: Open to more than one interpretation.
examples: His answer was deliberately ambiguous.
She was anxious about the interview.

awkward.monolingual: Causing or feeling unease; or clumsy.
examples: There was an awkward silence.

brilliant.monolingual: Exceptionally clever or talented; or wonderful.
examples: What a brilliant idea!
It was a challenging project.

confident.monolingual: Feeling sure of oneself or of an outcome.
examples: She's confident she'll win.
This step is crucial.

decent.monolingual: Conforming to accepted standards; or reasonably good.
examples: He's a decent guy.
It was a deliberate attempt to mislead us.
The city has a diverse population.

efficient.monolingual: Working productively with minimum wasted effort.
examples: She's a very efficient worker.

eager.monolingual: Wanting to do or have something very much.
examples: The students were eager to learn.

eventually.monolingual: After much delay or difficulty; in the end.
examples: He eventually agreed.
It was a fascinating book.

feasible.monolingual: Possible to do easily or conveniently.
examples: Is your plan feasible?
Handle with care — it's fragile.

genuine.monolingual: Truly what something is said to be; sincere.
examples: She has a genuine interest in art.
That's a harsh punishment.
The movie was hilarious.

humble.monolingual: Having a modest opinion of one's own importance.
examples: She's humble despite her success.
Change is inevitable.
What an intriguing question.

modest.monolingual: Unassuming in estimation of one's abilities.
examples: He's quite modest about his achievements.
It's obvious he's lying.

outstanding.monolingual: Exceptionally good; or not yet paid.
examples: She gave an outstanding performance.
The response was overwhelming.

powerful.monolingual: Having great power, influence, or strength.
examples: It's a very powerful engine.
Give me a precise figure.
My previous job was in marketing.
He was reluctant to leave.
She made a remarkable recovery.

reliable.monolingual: Consistently good in quality or performance.
examples: He's a reliable employee.

sensible.bilingual: (adj.) sensato, razonable (false friend: ≠ sensible en español)
sensible.monolingual: Practical and chosen with reason; not foolish. (False friend: not the same as Spanish 'sensible' = sensitive.)
examples: That's a sensible decision.

subtle.monolingual: So delicate or precise as to be difficult to perceive.
examples: There's a subtle difference.

thorough.monolingual: Complete with regard to every detail.
examples: We did a thorough cleaning.

tremendous.monolingual: Very great in amount, scale, or intensity.
examples: She did a tremendous job.

weird.monolingual: Suggesting something supernatural; or odd, unusual.
examples: That's a weird coincidence.

consequence.monolingual: A result or effect of an action.
examples: Every action has consequences.
His safety is my main concern.

attitude.monolingual: A settled way of thinking or feeling.
examples: I love your positive attitude.

issue.monolingual: An important topic or problem; or to officially distribute.
examples: We have an issue to discuss.
We're hoping for a positive outcome.
From my perspective, it's a great deal.

trend.monolingual: A general direction in which something is developing.
examples: What are the latest fashion trends?

achievement.monolingual: A thing done successfully with effort.
examples: Graduating was a great achievement.

advantage.monolingual: A condition that puts one in a favourable position.
examples: His size is a real advantage.
The main disadvantage is the cost.
